justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — California AB5/§226 requirements, Germany works council/BetrVG §87, Indonesia contractor classification with no legal transparency mandate. (2) Germany works council approval identified as a hard blocking pre-condition (BR-4, AC-2). (3) Driver classification data model difference explicitly identified — employee (CA) vs contractor (ID) with different field sets (BR-6, BR-7). (4) Formal escalation for legal review with structured options and recommendation (escalation type 'missing_context'). (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — ACs are jurisdiction-specific (AC-1 for CA, AC-2/AC-3 for DE, AC-4 for ID). (6) confidence.level = 45, well under the 60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough jurisdiction-specific analysis. Each of the three jurisdictions gets distinct business rules, acceptance criteria, and edge cases reflecting their unique legal regimes.
Works council approval modeled as a system-level hard gate (BR-4) with a concrete testable assertion involving feature flags and approval records, plus an edge case for approval revocation.

Driver classification difference clearly identified with concrete implications: CA drivers get hours_worked fields (BR-6), Indonesian drivers explicitly must NOT see employment-implying fields (BR-7).

Correctly identified the subtle trap: removing transparency features in Indonesia is fine, but removing them in California is illegal — captured via the invariant distinction (BR-3 invariant:true vs BR-7 invariant:false).
Business impact correctly identifies that global rollout cannot be uniform, citing jurisdiction-specific deployment gates and phased rollout necessity.
Confidence level of 45 is appropriately low, with well-reasoned justification citing the gap between the single-sentence spec and the multi-jurisdiction legal complexity.
Strong escalation structure: three distinct escalations covering real-time definition ambiguity, missing legal review, and undefined deduction taxonomy — each with options and recommendations.

Edge cases are excellent — cross-jurisdiction drivers, works council approval revocation, tip modifications, retroactive deductions, zero-delivery shifts under AB5, and GDPR erasure vs tax retention conflict.
Ubiquitous language includes AB5, Works Council (Betriebsrat), and Driver Classification — properly grounding the domain vocabulary in the multi-jurisdiction context.

grader_results.detail: field escalations[*].type equals check failed: expected 'ambiguity', got values: 'missing_context'
